name: Kunlun Mountain
shortDescription: Kunlun Mountain is a mythical mountain believed to be the dwelling place of deities and a source of immortality in Chinese mythology. It is often described as a paradise and the axis mundi, connecting heaven and earth.
description: Kunlun Mountain, or more accurately, the Kunlun Mountain range, holds a prominent place in Chinese mythology as a sacred and mystical location. It is not just a physical mountain, but a symbolic representation of the center of the world and the connection between the earthly and celestial realms. Often described as a paradise, it is said to be the dwelling place of numerous deities and immortals, most notably Xi Wangmu, the Queen Mother of the West. It's believed to be the source of the Yellow River and a place where one could attain immortality. Myths surrounding Kunlun Mountain depict it as a multi-tiered structure, often with layers of different environments and inhabitants. Gardens of jade, rare and powerful herbs, and fantastical creatures are said to populate its slopes. The mountain is also associated with the elixir of life and the peaches of immortality, both guarded by various mythical creatures and immortals. Access to Kunlun is difficult, requiring great virtue and often the intervention of divine beings. Emperors and heroes throughout Chinese history have sought to reach Kunlun in pursuit of immortality and enlightenment. The mountain represents a spiritual ideal, a place of purity, power, and connection to the divine. It is a recurring motif in Chinese literature, art, and philosophy, symbolizing the ultimate goal of spiritual transcendence and the harmony between humanity and nature. Stories depict it as a place of trials and tribulations, where only the worthy can ascend and achieve true immortality.

location.cosmological: Considered the axis mundi, connecting heaven and earth. Believed to be located in the west, though its exact location is often vague and mythical.

sources.title: Shan Hai Jing (Classic of Mountains and Seas)
sources.author: Unknown
